target: data/sections.json part1 — new Section 1.8 after Section 1.7 (The Gravity Paradox)
replacement_html:
1.8 The Refraction Cascade: When One Fix Breaks Everything

The dome needs aetheric refraction to reconcile flat geometry with observation (Section 1.5). But the refraction index n(r) forces the sun's physical altitude to vary seasonally from ~1,975 km (summer) to ~3,618 km (winter) — an 83% swing the model never accounts for. Every prediction that depends on the sun's physical position inherits this indeterminacy: angular diameters, tidal forces, eclipse geometry, and H(r)-derived formulae all break. The cascade does not touch claims that depend only on apparent position (analemma, crepuscular rays), and we say so explicitly.

Section 1.5 explains how aetheric refraction, n(r), is the dome's universal correction factor — it bends light to make flat geometry produce globe-like observations. That section examines the formula's internal problems: the fitted 0.20 coefficient, the formula-vs-table discrepancy, and the sun/firmament collision. This section examines a different consequence: what happens to the rest of the model when n(r) actually does its job.

The physical altitude problem

The dome states the sun's apparent altitude is 5,733 km. But n(r) varies radially — at the Tropic of Cancer (r ≈ 7,401 km), the sun orbits closer to the pole where n(r) is small; at the Tropic of Capricorn (r ≈ 12,614 km), n(r) is much larger. For the refracted apparent altitude to remain constant at 5,733 km everywhere, the physical altitude must compensate: lower where n(r) is small (summer, near the pole) and higher where n(r) is large (winter, far from the pole). Using the dome's own H(r) and n(r) formulae, the physical sun altitude works out to approximately 1,975 km at the summer tropic and 3,618 km at the winter tropic — an 83% seasonal variation.

This is not an exotic consequence. It is arithmetic: if the apparent altitude is fixed and the refractive index varies, the physical altitude must vary inversely. The dome model never specifies this physical altitude profile, never derives it, and never accounts for its downstream effects.

What breaks: physical-position predictions

Any dome prediction that depends on where the sun physically is — not where it appears to be — inherits the cascade. The affected predictions fall into a clear pattern: they use the sun's physical altitude or H(r)-derived geometry as input, and the 83% swing produces observable consequences the dome cannot explain.

WINClaimCascade EffectImpact
WIN-044Firmament Scaling FunctionFSF inputs include physical sun altitude; 83% variation makes the formula indeterminateVerdict flip: Misleading → Self-Contradicted
WIN-068Eclipse magnetic ensembleEclipse shadow position requires a definite physical sun altitude; cascade makes it seasonally ambiguousVerdict flip: Std Model Explains → Self-Contradicted
WIN-046Tidal S2 periodPhysical altitude modulates tidal force by 6.1× seasonally — 100× larger than observedCitation added (already Self-Contradicted)
WIN-056Solar elevation from H(r)Anchor WIN: this is where the dome uses H(r) to derive solar elevation — the cascade starts hereCitation added (already Self-Contradicted)
WIN-067Antarctic gravity holeGravity at rim depends on sun-altitude-dependent H(r); cascade adds second indeterminacyCitation added (already Self-Contradicted)
WIN-016Aberration refractive modeln(r) ≥ 1.2 at stellar radii displaces all star positions by degrees; Gaia data refutesSupplementary (already Refuted by Data)
WIN-017Parallax = firmament wobblen(r) wobble affects all observations, not just parallax; 1.8B Gaia stars refuteSupplementary (already Refuted by Data)
WIN-065Polaris systematic excessn(r) at polar radii still implies measurable stellar displacementSupplementary (already Refuted by Data)

What does NOT break: apparent-position observations

Predictions that depend on where the sun appears to be are unaffected. The analemma (WIN-018, WIN-019), crepuscular ray geometry (WIN-026), and sky-coordinate measurements all reference the apparent altitude of 5,733 km, which the cascade preserves by construction. We note this not as a concession but as an analytical distinction: physical-position predictions break because n(r) shifts the physical altitude; apparent-position observations survive because n(r) is what defines the apparent altitude. Naming the boundary makes the argument airtight.

The n(r) dilemma

The cascade also exposes a deeper inconsistency in the n(r) formula itself. At the sun's orbital radius (~6,700 km), the dome's formula gives n ≈ 1.235. A refractive index of 1.235 implies substantial bending for any ray passing through the medium — not just sunlight. Gaia DR3 measures stellar parallaxes to ~10 microarcseconds. For the aether to be consistent with Gaia, the effective refractive perturbation (n − 1) must be below ~10−7 at relevant radii. The dome's formula gives n − 1 ≈ 0.235 — eight orders of magnitude too large. The dome cannot simultaneously have n(r) large enough to bend sunlight by 6–29° and small enough to leave starlight positions undisturbed to microarcsecond precision. (A quantitative ray-trace through the dome's graded n(r) field would determine the precise stellar displacement; the qualitative impossibility is already clear from the magnitude comparison.)

The kernel of truth: Graded refractive index media are real physics — the Earth's atmosphere does bend light, and more strongly near the surface. The dome correctly identifies that a radially varying n(r) could in principle alter geometric predictions. What it misses is that the fix creates a cascade of secondary effects that are not observed and cannot be suppressed without introducing yet another correction layer. Section 1.5 examines the formula; this section shows that even if you accept the formula, the downstream consequences are fatal.
insertion_point: After the closing </details> of Section 1.7 (p1-gravity), before the Part 1b comment marker
holes_addressed: Gate 2 B1: Per-WIN audit — all 7 WINs re-examined, WIN-018 flip excluded per curmudgeon
Gate 2 B4: Numerics verified (83.2% modulation, 6.1x tidal, n=1.235 at sun orbit)
Gate 2 B7: Section 1.8 avoids collision with Section 1.7
Physical vs apparent position distinction explicitly named in prose
n(r) inconsistency stated qualitatively (no specific starlight bending angle)
Chapman retained as secondary for WIN-068 (noted in detail_verdict_text update, not section prose)
new_evidence_added: EXP-050-numerics.json: 6.1x tidal force seasonal variation (100x observed)
EXP-050-numerics.json: n(r)=1.235 at sun orbit vs Gaia 10 microarcsecond precision

anticipated_objections.objection: n(r) is wavelength-dependent — it bends sunlight more than starlight
anticipated_objections.response: The dome formula n(r)=1+0.20*(8537/H(r)-1) has no wavelength dependence. If the dome introduces one, it must specify the dispersion relation and show it produces 8 orders of magnitude difference between solar and stellar wavelengths. No known material does this.

anticipated_objections.objection: The physical altitude indeterminacy will be resolved in a future version
anticipated_objections.response: Until it is resolved, every H(r)-derived prediction is structurally indeterminate. The current version claims confirmed WINs from formulae that require a physical altitude it has not specified.

anticipated_objections.objection: The cascade only affects a few WINs
anticipated_objections.response: We explicitly limit the cascade to 8 WINs (2 flips, 3 citations, 3 supplementary) — not the entire model. The precision of the scope strengthens the argument.

anticipated_objections.objection: Apparent-position predictions prove the model works
anticipated_objections.response: We explicitly concede that apparent-position predictions are unaffected. The cascade applies only to physical-position predictions, and we name the boundary. This pre-empts the strongest counter-argument.

kernel_of_truth: Graded refractive index media are real physics. The Earth's atmosphere genuinely bends light, more strongly near the surface. The dome correctly identifies that a radially varying n(r) could in principle alter geometric predictions. The problem is not the concept but the magnitude and the downstream consequences.

verdict_changes.condition: Retain Chapman rebuttal as secondary in detail_verdict_text
